desc:
The wide road runs north-south for a great distance in both directions. The
western side of the road is bordered by a thick hedge of bushes and shrubs,
while to the east is an area of broad, grassy plains. Not far to the north, a
stone bridge crosses a gully of some sort.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road between Bree and Fornost stretches seemingly endlessly to the north and
south, a long trek that few but the most prepared undertake. Open plains and
distant hills stretch out in the east. The west, however, is blocked by dense
hedges, with a grim ruin looming to the northwest.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The long road to Fornost continues its journey north and south. To the east an
area of grassy plains stretches away for many miles while to the west the ground
is covered with clumps of bushes and the occasional oak tree. Not far from the
road to the west is a small, rickety-looking wooden bridge along a dirt trail.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The Road to Fornost is very wide and is leading to the south and to the north. A
steep slope makes further travel toward the west impossible while to the east
the top of a dark tower can be seen far away. The road shows signs of frequent
travel.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road is very wide here continuing to the north and to the south. A small
path leads west ascending a gentle slope toward several buildings while some
bushy fields extend to the east. Tracks of carts and horses can be seen in the
packed earth.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road continues its straight path to the north and south through plains.
Several large gouges in the road suggest that one or more horse carts were
tipped here. To the west a steep rocky slope makes further travel impossible.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road makes a long bend between some badlands to the southeast and a steep
slope on the northwest side. The badlands are scraggy but accessible for further
travel, however the road looks more welcoming. To the north the road continues
in a straight line for miles toward the horizon.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The ground beneath seems strong and well-used. The wide road makes a long bend
between hills and slopes. There are some wild forest weeds to the west that
could be hard to cross while to the north is a steep slope which makes further
travel in that direction impossible.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The route keeps on across Arda's meagre trees and plains. While badlands extend
to the east, it is impossible to travel further due to extensive undergrowth on
a steep slope to the west. The road reveals evidence of frequent use, such as
wagon ruts.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The route continues through a thin bush in both the north and south directions.
The normally relatively plain landscape can be seen rising above a number of
hills in the far north. Further travel to the west and east seems impossible due
to the sharply falling terrain.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
There are few to no turnoffs along the way, and the road stretches to the north
and south as far as the eye can see. The topography is marked by several sizable
hills to the northeast and a steep incline to the west, while a sharp drop to
the east prevents further travel.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
There are traces of frequent transit over a relatively wide route that runs both
north and south. A hill covered in trees can be found to the west, although
getting there is impossible due to the vegetation. There is no way to move east
due to an extremely steep slope.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
Here the road widens a bit to allow a more heavy flow of traffic. While the road
toward the north is leading through a sparse forest this part of the road is
completely out in the open. Bushy plains stretch out far to the east while a
steep slope to the west prevents further journey.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The Road continues its long journey north to Fornost and stretches both, north
and south, as far as the eye can see. To the east of the road bushy plains
continue for miles, while to the west a narrow trail ascends toward a sparsely
forested area.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
Tall trees sparsely cover the sides of this narrow trail, as it leaves the road
in the east and ascends toward the north. Briars and bracken cover the floor. To
the west is a clearing which can be seen through a gap between two old oaks,
while to the south the forest becomes denser.
name: Narrow Trail through the Hills

desc:
The road to Fornost passes between a dense forest to the west and an open field
to the east. The road's surface is slightly raised above the surrounding ground
to aid the runoff, and two shallow ditches run parallel to the road. Weeds and
other vegetation have wholly overgrown the ditches, and even grow between the
rock slabs that make up the road. A small track leads across the field to the
east, winding around a small building to the southeast.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The track extends east and west here amongst a grassy field that extends far to
the north and south. The ground here is mostly flat and free of rocks and roots.
Its surface is easy to walk on. Far to the south, a small wooden building sits
amongst tall grass.
name: Narrow Path

desc:
The road runs north-south from Fornost to Bree. Its monotonous journey is broken
briefly to the south, where a steep dip in the ground has been bridged by a
wooden structure. A wagon-trail leaves the road heading east, across the fields,
its hard-packed dirt a stark contrast to the carefully laid slabs of stone that
make up the road's surface.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
A wagon's trail winds its way between crop fields. A narrow strip of grass grows
between two thin tracks of beaten earth. The fields on either side of the track
look well tended, and a wide variety of crops are grown here. Far to the west, a
well-tended road runs between Bree and Fornost.
name: Trail to a Farm

desc:
A steep dip, only a dozen feet or so deep, cuts through the fields. A wooden
bridge spans the dip, and is wide enough to cross with wagons. The bridge has a
foundation of stone on either side of the dip, but the bridge itself is
constructed of sturdy wood.
name: On a Bridge

desc:
The road runs north-south from Fornost down to Bree. To the north, the road
crosses a dip in the lands by way of a wooden bridge, while to the south, the
road winds through green fields. The road's worn stone-slab surface is slightly
elevated above the surrounding fields; and two ditches, overgrown with rushes
and other vegetation, accompany the road on either side.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road passes through a landscape of sweeping fields and small hillocks
crowned with clumps of trees and bushes. A high fence separates the road from
fields of crop to the west. Worn, flat slabs of stone form the surface of the
road. Some weeds have managed to take root in places between the slabs,
oblivious of the constant threat of being trampled by foot or hoof, or crushed
beneath a wagon's wheels.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The Road to Fornost was built with great care and skill. The stone slabs that
form the road's flat, easily travelled surface are fitted with precision and
have stood the test of time excellently. The road takes a detour as it bends
around an area to the southwest with uneven ground and several large rocky
outcrops.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The road seems narrow here because it's squeezed between large, thorny bushes
that grow right up to the edge of the road on both sides. It appears that there
were ditches along the road at some point, possibly to aid with runoff, but
these are now barely discernible beneath branch and leaf, wild grass and weeds.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
This is an open area of grassy flatlands which stretch away to the south. The
old Fornost-Bree road lies not far to the west while to the north lies a narrow
ravine from where issues the sound of rushing water. To the east, the land rises
into some rolling hills.
name: Plains below the North Downs

desc:
In contrast to the bleak slopes further to the south, the hillside here is
populated by a great many trees of various sizes and shapes - perhaps a result
of the proximity of the river not far to the north. To the west the land dips
into an area of grassy plains, while to the east the undergrowth thickens at the
edge of a wild, untamed brush.
name: Wooded Hills

desc:
Wild, chaotic brushland grows at the southern shore of the river. Thorny vines
and broken branches cover the ground, lying amidst thin vegetation that seems to
have difficulties growing here. With visibility limited by the thick growth,
progression is difficult. Aside from the river to the north, a ridge of hills is
visible through the bushes to the west.
name: Bushy, Overgrown Riverbank

desc:
The land here is wide, flat and grassy, with few landmarks to guide the way. Not
far to the west lies the old Fornost-Bree road while to the east and south the
land rises into a swathe of rolling hills. The open plains continue to the
north.
name: Plains below the Downs

desc:
The rolling, low slopes of the North Downs continue to the north and south. The
land to the west descends into an area of grassy flatlands, beyond which a broad
road cuts across the land. Down a gentle slope to the east is the edge of a
thick and brambly brush.
name: North Downs

desc:
The occasional decaying tree trunk is visible through a sea of winding branches
in the midst of the dense brush. Wiry branches cover the ground, and the tall,
dense bushes block out most of the view of the sky. Progress through the thorny
terrain is extremely difficult.
name: Border of the Brushland

desc:
This small, wooden bridge seems very old. The planks are rotting and look ready
to break into pieces at any moment. Despite this, the bridge has clearly stood
for a long time. The elderly construction creaks faintly as it swings over a
narrow but deep ravine.
name: On a Creaking Bridge

desc:
Thin undergrowth creeps across the land, covering the trail in a thin layer that
does not completely hide it. To the east the path clears, showing a small bridge
and the long, wide road in the distance. The west and north, however, are deeper
into the brushland. The path snakes north, vanishing into the bushes.
name: Small Path in Some Bushes

desc:
The faint path runs between an ancient wall to the west and a wild, overgrown
hedge to the east. The trail runs straight, linking the thinning brush and road
to the south to deeper bushes in the north, beyond which creep the walls of an
eerie castle.
name: Small Path in Some Bushes

desc:
Tall grass marks the border of this brushland, with the fields to the south
giving way to thicker bushes here and to the north. The bushes spread out in a
dense sea to the north and east, growing uncontrollably. Low stone ramparts can
be seen far off to the north.
name: Bushes Bordering the Plains

desc:
This patch of dense vegetation is composed of small bushes, running between
crumbled remains of stone walls. Sticking branches grasp aimlessly at any who
pass by, drifting in a faint breeze. The wall to the west is impassable, though
the bushes continue treacherously to the north and south.
name: Wild Bushes

desc:
Curving from the east to the north, this winding path is almost completely
obscured beneath the sharp, green thornbushes. The bushes continue into the
southern wilderness, away from the creeping fortress along the path that
dominates the landscape.
name: Bending Path in Some Bushes

desc:
The path underfoot bends here, bounded by a short, mossy stone wall to the north
and thicker brushland to the east. Tall, waving branches limit visibility in
this area, growing ever denser to the west. Despite its remoteness, the trail
shows faint evidence of use.
name: Curved Path in Some Bushes

desc:
Landmarks vanish behind dense bushes that grow taller than a Man here. Prodding
branches threaten eyes and rough undergrowth is a constant tripping hazard.
There is the hint of a path to the left, and that is all that can be perceived.
To the south, the bushes somehow thicken.
name: Densely-obscuring Bushes

desc:
Savagely-thorned brush grows all around. So much like a natural hedge maze, the
bushes have crawled over ancient rubble and low, weathered stone walls to
enclose this bushy garden. The sky is still visible, though not much else is,
save for the hint of a passable gap to the north.
name: In a Thorny Holly

desc:
These are the remains of what must have once been a mighty drawbridge, spanning
the ravine below where the fast river flows. Time and battles have done their
work, however, and the mighty wooden beams are rotten and broken in many places.
The sound of rushing water can be heard from below, while to the north looms the
crumbling castle gate. A cold breeze whistles around the decaying stonework, but
no sound of living creatures can be heard.
name: Decaying Drawbridge

desc:
Here stands the gate of a long-ruined stone fortress, its archway collapsed and
walls ivy-clad. No gates bar this portal, the wood having long rotted away
leaving only the rusted remains of huge metal hinges. Heaped earth and fallen
stones are piled against the base of the walls, giving a once-proud
fortification the grim face of a barrow.
name: Gates of a Ruined Castle

desc:
The thorny brushland dips sharply to form this ravine. The muddy base of the
gully suggests that it was formed as run-off from the bushes above eroded the
soil, year after year. A short distance above, a creaking wooden bridge hangs
over the gap formed by the ravine, allowing travellers to avoid the obstruction
altogether. The sloping ground is only traversable to the north.
name: Muddy Ravine within the Thorns

desc:
Marram Fields continue for miles toward the south. The soil is very dry and no
trees can be spotted in the vicinity. The most prominent features of the
landscape are the vast marram grass cereals. To the east a steep slope makes
further travel impossible.
name: Marram Field

desc:
A vast field of marram grass cereals continues to the north. The field is a
gorgeous blanket of green sprinkled with wildflowers. Although the grass is
thick, it is still possible to move through the field. The wildlife becomes more
varied as you move south, and a steep slope to the east prevents further
movement.
name: Marram Field

desc:
Wild weeds that are several feet tall and have sharp edges blanket the ground
here, making it difficult to move about. The vibrant weeds are the aces of the
fields, as if when dealt by nature's hand they belong there in a way the
regimented rows of a garden can never do. The land is sparsely covered in low
trees. Their crooked branches reach upward in an effort to touch the sky.
name: Wild Weeds

desc:
This field is covered by weeds in a perfect triangular form. They are several
feet high and have slightly sharp edges. It is hard to travel here without
gathering a few scratches. The terrain is descending toward the south leading
into a secluded valley.
name: Wild Weeds

desc:
This small secluded valley is covered with dense vines and weeds making it
seemingly impossible to continue further. To the east several large boulders
cover the flank of the valley while the slope toward the north seems more
feasible to travel.
name: Secluded Valley

desc:
Light grass covers a wide expanse of rolling hills here. To the north is an area
of open, grassy flatlands, while to the east the hills continue. Beyond the
hills is a green sprawl of brushland. Not far to the west can be seen the a
large road which runs north-south.
name: North Downs

desc:
Smooth, gently sloping hills stretch away to the north and west, covered in a
thin layer of grass. To the east is a thick brush, its branches wild and thorned
and its tall bushes blocking the view of what lies past the border. Wide plains
open to the south as far as the eye can see.
name: North Downs

desc:
The thick brushland borders a sudden, sloping incline to the south and west.
Bounded by the hills, the brushes expand in all other directions, wild and
grasping with branches and thorns. Dense growth creates unsteady ground
underfoot, and winding vines wrap around each other, making for a treacherous
tripping hazard.
name: Bushy Base of the Hills

desc:
The bushes stretch out in all directions at the base of a slick, mossy slope
that descends from the north. The slope is too steep to climb, lacking in stable
handholds. Visibility is low, and nothing can be discerned about the brush
elsewhere.
name: Brushland of Fornost

desc:
Broad, old trunks stand amidst the wild bushes like ancient stone pillars,
casting the surrounding brush into shadow. A large pile of decaying leaves has
amassed at the base of a steep slope to the north. There is only more brushland
all around, though the way to the east is a dense tangle of branches that would
be hard to pass and harder to return through.
name: Brushland of Fornost

desc:
The depths of the brushland is a natural maze of branches, vines, and ancient
trees. While the thorny, grasping bushes continue all around, the way south is
unusually thick with flora. Undergrowth has grown tall over a fallen trunk,
making the way impassable.
name: Deep in the Brushland

desc:
A small pond of clean water is here, scattered leaves drifting on its surface.
The occasional root reaches out towards the water, but otherwise the area around
the pond is clear of branches, a rare opening in the otherwise thick chaos of
the brushes. The clearing halts at an eroded, mossy slope to the south.
name: Bushy Clearing

desc:
The soil is soft and damp near the river to the north, wild and uneven with
rough, winding roots. The full height of the brushes is hidden by the thick
growth of their branches, blocking out sight of the top of the brush. The brush
continues in all directions, though to the west the vines and branches are too
dense to navigate.
name: Brushland Bordering a River

desc:
The depths of the brushland are a natural maze of branches, vines, and ancient
trees. With only wild bushes in every direction, there are no landmarks to guide
an explorer's progress. A single point of interest is a broad mound of earth to
the north, shrouded beneath the dense undergrowth.
name: Heart of the Brushland

desc:
There are scattered trees and wild shrubs across this brushland. There is no
sign of water on the sluggish surface of a little dried-up streambed that goes
eastward. In the streambed's moist soil, mosquitoes can deposit their eggs
safely.
name: Soggy Brush

desc:
High grass grows in this large clearing which is surrounded by thick forest and
brush. The ground feels soggy making this the perfect habitat for wildlife. To
the south the bushes get very dense making further passage through the underwood
impossible.
name: Soggy Brush

desc:
This is the highest spot of the dry badlands giving a good view of the
surrounding lands. To the north the badlands decline toward brushlands covered
with wild vegetation while to the east the lands look soggier. A large tower
made of black stone is to the south surrounded by an old, hay-covered stonewall.
name: Badlands

desc:
In this eastern part of the downs the hills are expanding into wide open areas.
Westwards the rolling plains stretch all the way to the horizon. The grass that
covers the ground sways in the gentle breeze, almost looking like the waves on
the ocean. In the distance to the southeast a tower of black stone rises.
name: Windswept Downs

desc:
Gentle rolling plains stretch far to the east and south while toward the north
the downs get more undulating. To the west a frequently used road is leading
from north to south. Small groups of trees and bushes are scattered among the
plains as the perfect habitat for wildlife.
name: Windswept Downs

desc:
While the flora becomes denser in the north, the south opens up to wide rocky
plateaus. There aren't many trees here, but those that do flourish stretch their
limbs skyward. In these otherwise marshy regions, a number of waterways connect
smaller ponds.
name: Ponds on a Boggy Land

desc:
With the exception of a few reeds near a waterway, this open region is entirely
covered with grass and is devoid of any nearby plants or trees. The grass is
short, making movement easy. On the western badlands, a huge tower made of black
stones may be observed. There is no direct access to the west due to an old
stonewall.
name: Grassy Lands

desc:
Dry cracked plains covered with occasional tufts of dry grass are the only
plants visible. A cliff's edge to the west and south prevents further movement
in those directions. Excellent landscapes may be seen all the way to the horizon
in the direction of the south. A wide rocky plateau can be seen to the north.
name: Burned Plains

desc:
There are sporadic trees and parched grass on this rocky plateau. Muddy pools to
the east block further travel. At the surface of the murky pools,
dangerous-looking bubbles burst, leaving a sulphurous odour in the air. The
plains in the north and west appear to be rather marshy while to the south they
appear to be burned.
name: Edge of Boggy Lands

desc:
In the east and north, the plains become more humid, whereas in the west, the
land dries out. To the northwest, a huge black tower is encircled by an ancient
stonewall covered in hay. A treacherous rocky ledge to the south prevents
further movement. However, the view is stunning and plains can be seen all the
way to the horizon.
name: Pond between Reeds

desc:
An old, hay-covered stonewall surrounds the badlands to the north. Vines and
other vegetation are growing on the wall. A little section of the wall is cut
off by a large fissure in the eroding plains. The sole visible route to the
north is a dilapidated wooden arch bridge that was constructed at this fissure.
A tower composed of black stone is the badlands' most distinctive feature.
Except for an extremely steep slope of rubble that leads to the western
badlands, the rest of the terrain is dry and level.
name: Eroded Plains

desc:
The tall black tower to the north is slightly tilted to one side and its
battlements are twisted in shape and design. Vines have taken root along the
shaded areas of the tower and spiral-shaped thornbushes surround its base. At
the top of a flight of stairs is a large stone door leading into the tower. The
only way out of these isolated badlands, which are encircled by an old,
hay-covered stonewall, is to the south, where a wooden arched bridge spans a
deep fissure.
name: Black Tower on an Isolated Badland

desc:
A small path paves its way northward toward a tall black tower which can be seen
far on the horizon. The plains slightly ascend toward the south leading toward a
slightly higher hill while far to the west the plains change toward badlands.
name: Structured Plains

desc:
The depositioned plains are flanked by a steep cliff wall to the north and east.
The wall is composed of different looking layers of rock that make it impossible
to climb. The otherwise rocky plains continue to the west and south.
name: Depositioned Plains

desc:
The rocky plains run through a very wide valley which is flanked by a steep rock
wall in a general northeast direction and a rubble covered slope to the
southwest. Small rocks and boulders make for hard travel through the otherwise
grass-covered plains.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
These depositioned plains are flanked by a steep rock wall to the east and
north. At the top of the eastern cliff a small shabby wooden hut can be seen.
The otherwise rocky plains continue far toward the southern and western horizon.
name: Depositioned Plains

desc:
The extremely dry conditions and the resulting poor vegetation has taken its
toll on the land. A wide valley slightly ascends toward the northwest while to
the south more fertile lowlands continue far toward the horizon. A sandy, dry
slope leads toward higher lands to the east.
name: Structured Plains

desc:
Grassy plains continue in every direction while the lands slowly descend toward
the southeast into the rich green lowlands and to the north the colour changes
gradually from a healthy green to a more desolate brown. Apart from the grass
there is no special vegetation in the vicinity.
name: Grassy Lowland Plain

desc:
The lowlands around here are covered with a deep green grass and lots of bushes
and trees except of a small clearing of about sixty feet in diameter which is
free of any vegetation at all. In the centre of the clearing there is a large
thorny bush.
name: Lowland Clearing

desc:
The lowlands in this area are green and fertile however the ground is very
uneven and many small rocks cover the ground, hidden between high grass making
it hard to travel. Toward the west and south the undervegetation and bushes get
very dense, making further travel in that direction impossible.
name: Lowland Plain

desc:
Although dry the plains around here are covered with grass and widely spread
bushes. In a general northwest direction there is a steep slope covered with
loose rubble leading up toward a long stone wall. The plains ascending toward
the north look even more desolate while the other directions lead toward more
fertile lands.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
Fine particles of dust and sand cover the desolate plains which gradually ascend
toward the north, while the southeast is descending into lowlands. A steep slope
to the west makes further travel in that direction impossible. The plains toward
the south are getting more fertile and the vegetation gets greener.
name: Depositioned Plains

desc:
The rocky plains gradually ascend toward the north while to the west is a very
steep slope. The slope is covered with rubble, making it impossible to climb and
on the top is a long stone wall. Toward the southeast there is a large clearing
leading into the lowlands.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The ground is cracked and disjointed with numerous jagged edges and stone
outcroppings making travel difficult. A small path continues to the west along a
steep slope leading toward a long stone wall. To the east and north the rocky
plains continue.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
This huge field of grass stretches all the way to the horizon. A small trampled
path is leading through short grass toward a stone house to the south. The grass
beside the path has grown much higher and tall rocks make the passage
exhausting. In the northwest some hills are rising over the grass and show their
crests by rising high above the plains.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
A narrow strip of land covered by white sand is flanked by badlands to the west
and north. East of the narrow strip a barely green turf has managed to grow
while toward the south a small path paves its way between sandstone boulders
toward a cliff.
name: Eroded Plains

desc:
The plains stretch out towards the horizon in all directions here. The green
grass turning brown in the western parts where badlands dominate the view,
barren and dry. Only a few tracks can be found of the sparse wildlife, most of
them old and hard to make out.
name: Eroded Plains

desc:
The vast badlands give a good view of the surrounding lands. A wide and
well-frequented road which runs in a general north to south direction can be
seen to the west. To the north large rolling plains continue while to the east
and south the badlands decline steeply toward an eroded landscape. Large
boulders to the east seem to make further travel impossible.
name: Badlands

desc:
Trees are scattered all over the low rolling plains. The ground here is not as
fertile as to the north which makes it hard for the vegetation to grow. The road
just west of here is leading in a general north to south direction. To the east
and south the rolling plains turn into badlands covered with sand.
name: Windswept Downs

desc:
The badlands are covered with dry grass and sand. To the west and north rolling
plains continue while to the east and south a sudden steep and sandy elevation
change makes further passage impossible. A big black stone tower to the
northeast is encircled by an old hay-covered stonewall where vines and other
plants are growing.
name: Badlands

desc:
The vast, grassy flatland is dotted with a few trees and stretches out in all
directions as the surrounding scenery sings a lullaby in warm, nostalgic tones.
A broad road that appears to be well-travelled and heading about north to south
passes by to the west.
name: Windswept Downs

desc:
Rolling plains which are free from most kinds of vegetation continue far to the
north and west. To the south sand covered badlands rise over the horizon,
casting long shadows over the flat landscape. A large tower made of black stone
rises to the east. Between the plains and the tower an old, hay-covered
stonewall makes further passage impossible.
name: Windswept Downs

desc:
This small wooden hut which seems to be abandoned by its former owner has fallen
into shabbiness and disrepair. Cracks in the walls and roof are a sign that this
building has seen better days. The boards in the walls are loose and badly
battered. The entrance to the east is covered by a crooked door barely hanging
in its hinges. Scattered about the room are fragments of bones, bits of fur, and
a partly decomposed human corpse. It is obvious that the cabin remains in use
even though the original owner has departed.
name: Abandoned Hut

desc:
A small brook is here, winding through the fields. The water is so clear that it
is possible to see the bottom which is covered with round stones. The brook
seems to originate somewhere to the south but dense bushes make it impossible to
find its source. Fields can be seen west of here, and some bushes are to the
east.
name: Sparkling Brook

desc:
The grass on these fields are short and juicy making it perfect for cattle to
graze in. Bushes are scattered across the fields and an occasional tree can be
spotted. To the north the fields are spreading out and to the east a small brook
winds its way through the fields.
name: Brookside Field

desc:
Fields covered with lush green grass are spreading out to the north and east.
Some bushes are scattered around the fields and some trees that can provide
shade on a hot summer day can be spotted. To the south and west large bushes and
some trees can be seen.
name: Bushy Field

desc:
Here a large clearing opens up, making travelling comfortable. Short green grass
covers the ground. To the north dense bushes can be seen, to the west a small
trail winds its way through a sea of grass and to the east lush green fields can
be spotted. A trail towards a rugged animal track is leading south.
name: Clearing in a Sombre Brushwood

desc:
Here the fields are covered with high grasses that swirl in the wind. To the
west and south the grass seems to be shorter, thus making it easier to walk
through. Thick bushes to the north and east make travel in those directions
impossible.
name: Sombre Field

desc:
These fields are covered with lush green grass, that will sparkle in the morning
when the fog is chased away by the warm sun. A small trail is leading to the
north beside a dried-out brook toward wastelands while to the east the grass
continues. To the west and south the vegetation gets denser.
name: Sombre Field

desc:
Thick bushes grow here, making travel somewhat difficult. Some bird-chirping can
be heard from inside the thick bushes. The bushes seem to be passable to all
directions, but seem to be less dense to the north and east where grassy fields
can be seen through the thicket.
name: Brushwood near Grassy Fields

desc:
These infertile plains are covered with dry grass and some bushes which seem to
be the only plants that have been able to get a foothold in the dry soil. To the
east the wasteland spreads out, but to the west and south some lush verdure is
seen. To the north a very steep hillside with a long stone wall on its top makes
further journey in that direction impossible.
name: Dry Wasteland

desc:
Dry vegetation covers the fields, some dead trees are scattered across the land.
From east to south a dried-out brook winds its way, breaking the monotony of
this infertile landscape. A small trail is heading south, where some greenery
can be seen. To the west the wasteland continues while to the north a steep
hillside with a long stonewall at its top makes further journey in that
direction impossible.
name: Dry Wasteland

desc:
A path descends through grass and bushes toward the west while to the south the
bushes are too dense for travel and to the north a steep drop makes further
passage impossible. Down to the north a valley trough can be discerned while to
the east the dry plains continue.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
A small animal path weaves its way through the grass. A small brook murmurs
through the undergrowth of grass and some small bushes. The bushes seems to get
denser to the south, and to the east the plains continue to ascend out of the
valley.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
Dry plains with brown, tall grass cover the ground of the valley. Moving to the
east, a forest can be discerned. Tracks and trails of animals can be seen where
the grass is pushed and flattened. The valley slightly ascends toward the north
and descends to the south.
name: Dry Grasslands

desc:
The dry grassland stretches far into a valley in the south direction, covered
with dry grass. To the west and east the valley is flanked by steep and rocky
terrain making further passage impossible. To the north the dry grassland
ascends into sandy badlands.
name: Dry Grasslands

desc:
Small bushes and dry grass cover the grounds of the badlands and no shelter can
be seen in the near vicinity. A large road which runs in a general north to
south direction makes a long bend around these hills. To the south the plains
continue while to the east the badlands decline steep toward an eroded
landscape.
name: Badlands

desc:
The trail is ascending toward the west while to the east it joins into a very
wide road. The northern and southern slopes ascend steeper and a stone wall
makes further passage there impossible. To the west several buildings made out
of stone can be seen.
name: Trail toward an Abandoned Farm

desc:
Stone ruins of an abandoned farm can be seen at the end of the trail. Toward the
north is an uncultivated ranch while to the south farmlands can be seen.
Everything seems to be abandoned and where once surely was a thriving farm now
only ruins remain.
name: Abandoned Farm

desc:
Although abandoned, this ranch is still very impressive because of its sheer
size. Still intact stone walls and steep slopes beyond them border the land. A
big gate to the south leads toward the ruins of a farm and the ranch extends far
toward the east.
name: Abandoned Ranch

desc:
Old stone walls prevent further movement in the opposing directions as the
abandoned property extends for miles to the west. A swing makes a musical
creaking as if it has become both entertainer and audience over the years.
Large, rocky badlands may be seen in the very distant east, and a river in the
very far north.
name: Abandoned Ranch

desc:
The abandoned farmland must have once served as a source for local produce but
now the barren soil can only grow occasional weeds and scrubs. Stone walls
border the infield and to the north a large gate leads toward the ruins of a
farm.
name: Abandoned Farmland

desc:
Barren farmland stretches for miles toward the west while in all other
directions a stone wall makes further travel impossible. A long road leading
toward the south can be spotted from up here while to the east vast badlands
continue toward the horizon.
name: Abandoned Farmland

desc:
These ghastly plains are covered in an extremely thick layer of purple weeds
that resemble tentacles. The weeds wrap themselves around occasional rocks as a
lover holds the hand of her beloved. In the spirit of adventure that the growing
season provides, they yearn to climb and bloom, to decorate and love, and to
seize the day. The plains continue to the west and south, and possibly to the
east, a road's faint shape can be made out.
name: Grisly Plain

desc:
Different kinds of vines and weeds cover these grisly plains. It is hard to find
a path through this thicket of vines with sharp edges and thorns. Toward the
east and west the thicket gets denser and further travel seems impossible.
name: Grisly Plain

desc:
This valley is barren, save for a high slope to the east that is covered in
vines and weeds with jagged edges. There is no way to go any further in that
direction due to the slope's severe incline. North the terrain slightly ascends
toward plains with denser vegetation while to the south higher hills promise a
better view.
name: Barren Valley

desc:
The Road to Fornost, which is located far below at the bottom of a precipitous
descent, is clearly visible to the east from this modest overlook on top of a
forested hill. To the west, a simple wooden forest shelter with a cloth-covered
door rests in between some trees. Small gaps in the thicket allow for further
travel to the north and south, but the path westward beyond the shelter is
impossible.
name: Lookout on a Forested Hill

desc:
Tall trees encircle and hang over this small, protected glade in the forest.
Although no small animals are visible, there are quiet rustling noises coming
from the underbrush that indicate their existence. Only minor openings to the
west and north allow for further movement, while the east and south are heavily
overgrown, steeply descending, and hence impassable.
name: Forested Hill

desc:
The trough valley ends at the valley step where steep slopes make further travel
impossible. The valley is barren with only rocks and larger boulders covering
the floor. The occasional bush on the steep slopes is the only vegetation
visible.
name: Valley Step

desc:
Beneath the trees the floor is covered with bracken and brambles. To the east
there is a narrow path ascending up a hill which is covered by trees. A narrow
trail continues to the south but ends somewhere in the undergrowth to the west.
name: Dead-end Trail

desc:
Here the trail dips into a small hollow as it bends from east to north. The
trees grow more sparsely here and meadows with the occasional bush dominate the
fauna. The ground is somewhat damp and mossy, while the air is cool and has a
musty smell.
name: Bend in a Narrow Trail

desc:
The narrow trail continues to descend to the west and south while to the north
and east the undergrowth becomes too dense to penetrate. Through a small gap
toward the east a small part of the Road to Fornost and the bushy plains beyond
can be seen. An ancient stump stands beside the path, from which small fungi are
growing.
name: Narrow Trail through the Hills

desc:
Heathlands with a low-growing woody vegetation stretch toward the east where the
tangle becomes dense and impassable ascending on a hill while to the north
suddenly a deep drop into a valley makes further passage impossible. The only
passable footpath is toward a sparsely forested area south.
name: Hidden Heathland

desc:
The small wooden shelter provides cover from natural influences. Bushels of
straw lie by the northern wall and are partially covered by a blanket. A small
table and chair in the centre of the room and a cupboard on the backside are the
only visible pieces of furniture. The shelter is lit by a candle that has been
placed on the cupboard.
name: Forest Shelter

desc:
Small sandstone boulders give the land some shape and shelter the few plants
that have managed to grow here. This seems to be a narrow strip of land leading
to a cliff covered by white sand to the south. At the edge of the cliff there is
a steep drop to the valley below.
name: Depositioned Plains

desc:
The house made out of white stone blocks consists of one large room. A simple
stone fireplace is built into the western wall connected to a chimney. Right in
front of the fireplace is a small wooden table and toward the east side of the
room a cosy bed is set up against the wall. There are two visible exits out of
the house, one to the north and one leading south onto the large veranda. The
room gets plenty of natural light during the day thanks to small windows on the
house's northern and southern sides. In order to light the space at night,
candles are placed next to the fireplace.
name: Goatherder's House

desc:
The wooden hut is built out of wooden logs and consists of one big main room. In
the centre of the room is a large table while toward the southern wall there is
a bed and a cabinet used as storage. At the far end of the room is a small
stove.
name: Wooden Hut

desc:
A thick brush covers the trough of the valley. The surrounding flanks are steep
and covered with rock and boulders making further passage out of the valley
impossible. At the end of the trough is a small hut made of wooden logs.
name: Thick Brush at a Valley Trough

desc:
The grass and small bushes that make up this quiet meadow is bordered by
tangled, impassable shrubs to the east, and a steep drop to the west. The open
meadow continues along a low ridge to the south. In the north, the brush and
trees grow thicker.
name: Quiet Meadow on a Low Ridge

desc:
This long ridge is covered with green grass and sparse but thriving
glossy-leaved trees. It descends gently towards the south where it meets a
winding, narrow track that crosses the fields from west to south-east. A dense
wall of bushes follows the ridge closely to the east.
name: Low Ridge

desc:
The track bends this way and that, moving around and in-between obstacles to
take the easiest route through these fields. The path winds deftly around
several large boulders, and stands of hazel. The brown path is beaten, trampled
earth, and too narrow for wagons.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
Thorny bushes grow in dense clusters, with barely enough space between them to
move across these fields. Towards the west, a low ridge barely rises above the
top of a row of tall, thorny bushes. Thick roots snake across the ground, making
footing unsure.
name: Dense, Tangled Bushes

desc:
Knobby roots, thorny thistles, and heavy brush cover the terrain here. To the
north and east the undergrowth is so thick that any passage seems impossible.
While to the west and south the bush is slightly less rampant, making the land
somewhat more navigable.
name: Dense, Tangled Bushes

desc:
This area is overgrown with various bushes, many of which grows taller than a
man on horseback. There is little to be seen in any direction except bushes of
all sorts and sizes, and they grow so dense that they completely block travel in
all directions except to the south where they grow a little thinner.
name: Tangled Dead End

desc:
In all directions the way forward is obstructed by tangled bushes, their
branches bound by winding, climbing vines. The earth is loose and scattered
about, filling the air with its scent, leaving clear evidence that wild boars
were here rooting among the bushes. The bushes grow more sparse to the south.
name: Dense, Tangled Bushes

desc:
The dense shrubbery and overgrown thornbushes fill the air with their heavy and
earthy scent. To the south and west, the mass of flora is impassable for even
the smallest of hobbits. Flowers can be seen poking through to the north and
east, where the bushes are more sparsely-distributed.
name: Dense, Tangled Bushes

desc:
A great many bushes and shrubs grow on these fields, often so close that they
form veritable walls of vegetation. The ground is hidden beneath a treacherous
layer of leaves, thick, snaking roots, and dead branches. Several stands of
hazel rise above the lower bushes.
name: Corner in Some Tangled Bushes

desc:
Between the few bushes that grow here, the ground is covered by grasses, forbs,
and dead leaves. The land descends here from the gnarly bush-tangled wilderness
in the north and cradles a small south-flowing stream into a wide valley between
the steep, boulder-strewn hills in the east and west. An occasional tree graces
the mostly-open landscape.
name: Gentle Slope along a Creek

desc:
Rushes grow here near the creek that splashes over stones and flows swiftly down
the hill from north to south. The ground rises again to the east across the
creek, where large, jagged boulders pierce the smooth grassy slope and intrude
into the sky. Songbirds occasionally fill the air with their music.
name: Gentle Hillside near a Creek

desc:
The open hillside here is populated primarily by countless clumps of grass
interrupted by a few isolated trees. To the south and west the upward slope
becomes too steep for travel, guiding the creek to flow eastward toward a
cluster of large rocks barely visible in the distance. Rushes and reeds share
the soft banks of the creek with many smooth, flat stones.
name: Creek on a Gentle Hillside

desc:
Dense thickets at the edge of a steep slope guide the stony track to the west
and south. The sky is open above, and small weeds have found purchase between
the rocks underfoot, suggesting that this path is seldom travelled. Evidence of
wildlife is observable among the flora beside the trail, which occasionally
rustles as something unseen moves through it.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
The faint path is overgrown with clumps of grass and forbs here, where it winds
around and between many large rocks. Fallen leaves from a few small rowan trees
growing among a cluster of boulders lie scattered about in various stages of
decay. To the west and south, the relatively flat, bouldered meadow near the
trail is swallowed by an impenetrable wild rose briar that covers the steep
hillside beyond.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
A vague path leads through the boulder-strewn wilderness. In some places it is
very hard to discern, since the dense underbrush seems to cover parts of it.
Quite a few old willow trees surround this narrow track, leaning in over it with
their crooked branches. The rocky ground drops abruptly east of the trail and
tumbles down to a green valley in the distance. The way to the west is blocked
by an impenetrable wild rose briar.
name: Narrow Track

desc:
The path continues north and south from here as it wraps around the side of a
hill that slopes down from the northeast to the southwest. Tall deciduous trees
grow among the boulders, and the trail is difficult to discern under old dead
leaves. An old, scrawny willow stands at a bend in the hillside, and the trail
splits in two and flows around it.
name: Narrow Track

desc:
A narrow pathway consisting of packed earth and occasional stone markers
meanders through fields of grass and hay. To the north there is an inhospitable
mass of thornbushes and other shrubbery, creeping ever so slowly towards the
man-made path.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
Fields of grasses and forbs surround this narrow track as it meanders through
the wild and uninhabited countryside. The hard path of trampled earth is beaten
and too narrow for wagons. A few trees can be seen sprouting along the trail as
it winds and bends.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
This narrow, stony trail cuts through the wild waves of grasses and short
woody-stemmed flowering plants that grow here. Clumps of grass encroach on the
hard-packed dirt and small half-buried rocks underfoot. The trail bends north
and east around the trunk of an ancient willow tree with wide branches.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
Surrounded on all sides by fields of long grass and weeds, a wide, rarely
travelled path meanders through the countryside. The ground on the path appears
to have had some use in the past, but the long grass is slowly starting to grow
through the hard-packed dirt. A handful of trees can be seen in the distance,
but they appear to be stunted and very old.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
A track here flows across the fields, bending and twisting to avoid any obstacle
and terrain that is less ideal for walking or riding. The land is wild and
uninhabited, except for some crop fields and farmsteads in the distance to the
south-west. Long grasses grow along the sides of the track.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
There is a small hollow here, in which grows an array of wildflowers that almost
seem to have been placed there on purpose, long ago. The remains of a campfire
can be found in the midst of a sea of colours, perhaps proof of the calming
nature of the hollow. The chirps of birds fill the area with an orchestra of
competing sounds.
name: Small Hollow

desc:
This trail runs across a field wedged between dense brush to the north and a
building to the south-west. The trail is little more than a partly overgrown
path, not wide enough for wagons. Some dung along the side of the trail may
indicate that the path is at least sometimes used by riders.
name: Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
Bushes are making travelling here somewhat tiresome. Toward the north the plains
get bushy and thorny while toward the south grassy plains and a narrow track can
be seen. Small animals run around in the dense underbrush and birds can be heard
chirping.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Here, east of the Road to Fornost, bushy and thorny plains stretch out towards
the eastern horizon. The soil is dark and nutritious, making the brush dense and
full of life. Many tracks can easily be found on the ground, running beneath the
carpet of vegetation.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Green and dense bushes are making travelling here somewhat tiresome. Sometimes
the noise of small animals can be heard from within the shrubs. These plains
continue in all directions except toward the north where dense underwood makes
further travel impossible.
name: Sombre Brushwood

desc:
These plains covered by sombre brushwood continue in all directions except
toward the south where dense bushes make further travel impossible. The soil is
dark, making the brush dense and full of life. There are many tracks of animals
on the ground.
name: Sombre Brushwood

desc:
Burnt down trees are the only remainder of the forest that once covered these
lands. The soil is still black from the ash created by the fire that burned the
forest to the ground. Vegetation and wildlife have not found their way back to
this land.
name: Burnt Forest

desc:
Once cleared by fire to prepare it for farming, this piece of land seems to have
been left uncultivated, and is far from suitable for farming in its current
state. Charred, decaying tree stumps still spot the ground amid clusters of
thick vegetation. A few young trees have survived here, harbingers of the forest
bordering this burned land to the south and east.
name: Burn-beaten Land

desc:
A smattering of young trees with straight, narrow trunks grow here, where
natural succession is reclaiming the burned land for the forest. To the south
and east the trees are larger and grow more densely, with broad trunks and
gnarly branches. Past fires have painted black bands around the trunks of the
few remaining older trees.
name: Edge of a Young Forest

desc:
The grass on this wide meadow grows above knee-height in some places, and shows
signs of grazing in others. Clusters of brush and inedible plants suggest that
this field is not currently being maintained as a pasture, but may once have
been part of the farmland to the south. Trees and dense vegetation grow across
the meadow to the north and east.
name: Wild, Grassy Meadow

desc:
Here the grass is low and trampled, and the numerous animal tracks suggest that
it is regularly grazed. An old, weathered barn stands to the east, with fresh
manure piled up against the side facing this field. The land looks less
well-tended to the north and east. The field to the south appears to be used for
growing crops.
name: Expanse of Trampled Grass

desc:
Food crops grow here in long, slightly-raised rows of healthy-looking soil. This
field is surrounded by more like it that have been planted with different crops.
Tall wooden fences and well-used dirt paths separate each plot from its
neighbours. At the northeast edge of the cropland stands an old, weathered barn,
beyond which the cultivated farm is overtaken by brush. More buildings can be
seen beyond the fields to the south.
name: Between Cultivated Rows

desc:
Kept neatly apart from the potato field to the north, this grain field is
dedicated to the cultivation of wheat. The delicate plants have been sown in a
meticulous order, as if the farmer were planting a garden. The proud wheat
plants grow tall, suggesting that most travellers choose a different route and
the occasional visitors are mostly careful not to tramp about.
name: Wheat Field

desc:
The land has been heavily grazed, and patches of dry earth can be seen between
clumps of low, brown crabgrass and spots of clover. Dust rises with the
occasional gust of wind and hovers low to the ground. In the distance to the
east, a small house and paddock appear low on the horizon.
name: Thoroughly-grazed Range

desc:
The forage in this area gives the land a two-toned hue; nearer to the ground,
the stalks of the wheatgrass appear darkly green, while the chevron-patterned
spikelets at the head of the stalk have a light, golden tinge. Evidence of
cattle, sheep, and other grazing ungulates can be seen among the tall grass.
name: Wheatgrass Pasture

desc:
The farmer who planted this field of grain has kept it completely without
diversity. This plot is completely dedicated to the cultivation of rye. Line
after line, all as straight as arrows, the rye grows with barely a weed to be
seen within its depths.
name: Field of Rye

desc:
As if the farmer's patience or energy ran out keeping the fields to the south
and west in their splendid shape, this one must have been left to itself for
years. What may once have been fields of crop is now nothing but weeds and wild
grass. A path to the barn to the north is now completely overgrown.
name: Overgrown Field

desc:
This once may have been a yard or at least an open field. It has long since been
reclaimed by nature, and brush covers most of it. A decrepit old barn to the
west looks equally abandoned and untended. The brush grows denser and taller
towards the north, and beyond it a canopy forms an even taller wall of
vegetation. The space in front of this barn may once have been cleared, but it
has long since been reclaimed by nature, and is overgrown with brush.
name: In Front of a Decrepit Barn

desc:
Mature hardwoods grow here where their fallen leaves form the soft forest floor.
There are trees of all sizes. Large fallen trunks lean askew here and there,
some sharing the ground with a few bushes and vines. The view through the forest
is mostly clear, and sometimes wildlife can be observed.
name: Mature Hardwood Forest

desc:
Here the forest floor is a jumble of rotted wood and decaying leaves. Vast
interwoven roots emerge from the earth like sculptures of wood and mud. The
scent of flowers mingles with the rank, humid soil and the rotting vegetation. A
heavy silence holds the the forest in its grasp, interupted only occasionally by
a bird's chirping or some forest animal causing the undergrowth to rustle.
name: Dense Mixed Forest

desc:
This seems to be the northern edge of the forest, as it thins considerably along
the northern fringes. The ground is covered with needles and fallen leaves and
the occasional dry, decaying branch. The forest floor show signs aplenty of
wildlife, especially evident are signs of wild boar that have grubbed around
looking for roots and such.
name: Thinning Edge of a Dense Forest

desc:
A thick layer of dust coats most of the surfaces inside this old barn, where
cobwebs adorn the spaces between heavy wooden beams and rusted farm tools. Along
one wall are animal stalls with gates allowing egress into an attached outdoor
pen. A workbench stands in a corner against the western wall, opposite the large
door. The building seems sturdy enough to provide relief from the weather
outside, but seems not to have been well-maintained for some time.
name: Dusty Barn

desc:
Three chairs stand around a small, scrubbed table that sits in the middle of
this small kitchen. An open cupboard is overflowing with cups, pots, and pans. A
caped greatcoat and several long scarves hang on the back of the door on the
northern wall. Small rugs lie like stepping stones on the scoured tiles. The air
reeks of beeswax, kitchen soap, and freshly ironed sheets.
name: Well-kept Kitchen

desc:
This dining room is sparsely decorated with just a few simple tapestries hanging
on the otherwise bare walls. A crudely crafted but functional table stands in
the middle of the room, surrounded by a handful of equally basic but functional
chairs. Small alcoves along the north wall seem to serve as places to bed down
for the night.
name: Dining Room

desc:
This paddock is enclosed by a shoulder-high wooden corral, its only exits a gate
to the west, and a wooden door to the north. The full and rich grass here is
trampled deeply in many places, suggesting it normally hosts a number of hoofed
animals. Large clumps of animal dung provide a rich feeding ground for beetles
and insects.
name: Lush Paddock

desc:
Tall weeds lie far to the south, and a fenced paddock to the east might house
farm animals. This meadow is very lush, and the grass is thick and rich. A large
rope swing hangs from the thickest branch of an old apple tree; yet it appears
to be unusable since one of its ropes is torn.
name: Lush Meadow

desc:
This pasture has clearly been grazed by cattle and the sounds of soft lowing can
be heard from quite a distance on this flat landscape. A shallow gully separates
this pasture from a trail to the south. A thicker meadow appears a short
distance to the east where the tiny forms of other livestock can be glimpsed,
just barely.
name: Pasture along a Trail

desc:
The grasses are lush and vibrant in this area. Bright green, tall fescue and
sturdy sorghum grow in abundance, providing plenty of forage for the livestock
that graze this land. A few willows dip their branches over the edge of the
gully to the south. The low trill of crickets vibrates through the air,
punctuated occasionally by the piercing chirp of a nearby chipmunk.
name: Lush Grassland near a Mill

desc:
Strips of shorn wheat sporadically dimple the field, and the breeze bears the
scent of freshly cut grass. A close look at these lately shorn areas reveals
small burrows and every so often, a tiny vole pokes its furry, blunt nose out,
whiskers atwitch. A calm quietness pervades the field as the grasses rustle in
the wind and the infrequent sound of sheep bleating echoes in the far distance.
name: Lightly-grazed Field

desc:
Bright green and golden grasses grow in large patches interspersed between the
occasional strip of bare dirt. Scorpion grasses add a splash of pale blue to the
ground cover. Overhead, the occasional flock of sparrows take wing, contrasting
darkly against the wide sky. To the south, a gully forms an abrupt dip in the
level ground.
name: Field beside a Trail

desc:
Upturned soil extends to the north in long, uniform rows, and the bare earth in
this area becomes charred ground to the south. Rounded bales of hay mark the
western edge of this fallow field, and to the east, spots of colour, greens and
yellows, relieve the eye of this drab, monotonous scenery.
name: Fallow Field

desc:
The ground here is uneven and several rocky outcrops explain why the Road to
Fornost takes a detour north and east around this area. Allowed to grow wild,
these fields are covered with dense brush and in parts they are almost
impassable. To the west, the fields are bordered by a small farmhouse, and a
solidly constructed wooden fence blocks the way there.
name: Brushy Field off the Road to Fornost

desc:
The air inside is heavy, the smell of rotten wood having seeped into the stone
walls and the damp earth. A thick wooden post extends from the centre of the
room upward, and small heaps of grain sacks line the western wall. Four lanterns
hang from hooks in the stone, left there by the inhabitants of this tower.
Toward the back end of the mill, a surprisingly sturdy wooden staircase ascends
to the second level, disappearing into the wooden ceiling.
name: Inside a Run-down Mill

desc:
The wooden post from the lower level extends upward into a hole in the ceiling,
straddled on both sides by two giant millstones. Specks of ground wheat are
scattered lightly around the millstones, but otherwise the floor is free of
debris. Dim light peeks through slight gaps in the ivy that covers the windows
on the outside.
name: Upper Level of a Mill

desc:
Dark stains of an unknown substance speckle the myriad of junk in this cramped,
moist cellar. Old, ratty clothes are mixed with an assortment of spider webs and
mouse droppings. The dirt ground underneath the thick layer of debris is slick,
and has helped cake the detritus with a thick layer of red and brown.
name: Moist Cellar

desc:
The hills lay low and fall away smoothly into the surrounding fields. At the
foot of the easternmost hill, a line of hay bales mark the edge of the farming
lands that extend east to the old, overgrown trail. Stubby grasses cover the
ground and a few, short cypresses appear as little more than greenish lumps in
the distance.
name: Cluster of Low Hills

desc:
All alone on an otherwise flat and even field of green grass stands an ancient,
majestic oak tree. It spreads wide branches in a wide circle around a trunk
covered with rough bark. The grass underneath the dense coverage is considerably
sparser than on the rest of the fields. There are signs of grazing in some
areas, and cowpats lie here and there attracting swarms of flies.
name: Solitary Oak in a Field

desc:
The fields here border a large farm to the west. The farm is surrounded by a
high and sturdy fence, meant to keep trespassers out, and its animals in.
Occasionally cows' moos and the neighing of horses are heard. To the south and
east the fields become wilder.
name: Outside a Farm

desc:
A field of low-lying grass covers the land here, with tufts of long grass
scattered almost haphazardly throughout it. In the distance, a rooftop can be
seen, rising above the horizon. The smell of smoke is faint, and occasionally
wafts by, carried through by the wind.
name: Wild Field

desc:
This field has potential and was clearly intended to be used to grow crops. It
is fenced in towards the east and south and paths connect it to cultivated
fields to the west. Nothing seems to have been grown here in recent years and
large bushes and weeds have begun to take over, making it ever harder to reclaim
as a farmland.
name: Untended Field

desc:
These open fields make up a rolling landscape, covered in healthy, green grass
and punctuated by the occasional tree or bush. Wildflowers of all types and
colours are scattered throughout the countryside, swaying in concert with the
wind, and filling it with their various smells.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The relatively flat ground begins to slope upward to the northeast, where an
impenetrable rose briar has claimed the boulder-encrusted hillside. Toward the
east and south, a wild meadow of tall grasses and wildflower plants is
interrupted only by the occasional tree. Birds and insects can be observed in
the undergrowth.
name: Wild Meadow

desc:
The wild meadow continues to the north and south. The tall grasses and forbs are
interrupted by dense clusters of bushes to the west. In the east, the terrain
becomes steep and rocky as it ascends a hill. An impenetrable rose-briar grows
on most of the hillside and provides shelter to small animals.
name: Wild Meadow

desc:
Within tall tufts of grass, a long, snaking depression in the land hints at a
dried out brook. Its dusty ground hasn't seen much water for a long time, as its
soil is cracked, and brittle. Tall ferns grow along its bank, and on the bottom
of the former brook are pebbles of all sizes, half-decayed fishbones, and other
detritous.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
A long depression in the land hints at a dried out brook. The brook's bed looks
like it haven't seen water for a long time, as its dirt is cracked and chipped.
Ferns and shrubs grow on what was once the brook's banks, and various weeds are
busy reclaiming the bed itself. Perhaps a hundred years from now this will look
like an overgrown ditch.
name: Dried-out Brook

desc:
These grassy fields look undisturbed and untamed, as if they have never felt the
bite of a plough. Tall grasses bend with the wind, forming green waves that
sweep across the plain. There is little to take note of here, although the high
grass could hold any kind of surprise below it, be it a venomous snake, or a
hidden pit.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
There are signs of a dried-out spring, and an equally dried out little lake next
to it. A small brook seems to have flowed to the west, snaking across the
plains. All that remains now is a grass-covered depression as the water probably
found another outlet and was directed elsewhere.
name: Source of a Dried-out Brook

desc:
These fields are wild and untamed, with the grass growing waist-high on a grown
man. The high grasses sway under the slightest breeze, sending green waves
across the flat landscape. The field is bordered to the north by a
sturdy-looking fence, beyond which crops are grown on more tamed land.
name: Field of Swaying Grasses

desc:
Wagon tracks lead up to the entrance of a farm just to the east, and the
trampled grass here indicates that it is in regular use. A long fence continues
to north and south, and the sturdy wooden gate is the only visible entrance into
the farm.
name: In Front of a Farm

desc:
These fields are dedicated to the growing of corn. Several fields, each with a
fence around it, consist of perfectly sown lines of corn, and nary a weed has
been allowed to take root. Wagon tracks run between the fields and seem to lead
towards the north.
name: Fields of Corn

desc:
Muddy tracks cover this foyer. A staircase climbs up the south wall. Underneath
the staircase, a boot rack holds boots of various sizes. In the north, the foyer
transitions into a kitchen while a door leads into the rest of the house to the
west.
name: Foyer of the Farm House

desc:
A sturdy table fills most of this little kitchen. A wood-burning stove rests
against the window, and the air is filled with the smell of grains. The walls
are hung with old knives, spoons, and pans, and from the ceiling hang loose bags
of potatoes, turnips, and onions. The wood floor creaks and is slippery with
flour dust.
name: Warm and Cosy Kitchen

desc:
A fireplace roars in the northeast corner of this cosy living room. Around the
fireplace are a couple rocking chairs and a couch. Next to the fireplace, a door
opens into a little bedroom. Smooth wooden planks are covered in the middle by
large, pink and white rug.
name: Living Room

desc:
A small, grassy area is enclosed by a small fence, just high enough to keep
children in and animals out. The grass is littered with toys, and numerous
tracks of tiny feet are stamped into the dirt. Towards the western side of the
yard, an old wooden ladder has been nailed to a small table, forming a crude
play structure.
name: Grassy Backyard

desc:
This bedroom is cramped, being mostly filled with a bundled-straw bed covered in
woolly blankets. A few toys are scattered across the floor, and along the
western wall there are two small, red chests filled with clothes and quilts. The
air is stuffy and hot, and the ceiling low.
name: Cramped Child's Room

desc:
Slanting walls narrow to a point in the middle of this attic bedroom. A large
bed is squeezed next to a brick chimney rising to the wood slats of the roof.
Hanging on the northern wall is a large quilt, pink and faded. On the south wall
hangs an ancient halberd and shield, nailed over an old grey blanket.
name: Large Bedroom

desc:
This is a small room filled with boxes of old clothing, unused toys, and linens.
In the corner is stuffed a torn painting, a mop, empty bottles, and two old
brooms. The ceiling is low, exposed wood slats, brittle, and brown. The floor is
made of rough wooden planks, the nails showing their coal black heads.
name: Closet

desc:
A small, grassy area is enclosed by a short fence, which curves around from the
northern side of the house to the west, then south. The grass is littered with
toys, and numerous tracks of tiny feet are stamped into the dust. In the middle
of the yard is a tall oak tree with a rope swing hanging from it.
name: Small Backyard

desc:
In the corner of this neat yard is an old stone well, the grey rocks covered in
smooth, bright green moss. The crank, bucket, and chain are newly built, crude
but functional. A low fence blocks access to the rest of the farmyard and a side
door leads into the barn to the east.
name: Old Stone Well

desc:
This barn is constructed of exposed, roughly hewn wood, hard and old. Underfoot,
interlocking, natural limestones form a flat, easy to shovel surface. The air is
thick with the smell of barn animals and fresh fodder. A rope ladder leads up to
the loft, which extends over the rest of the barn in the south. To the west, a
side door, too narrow for a large animal to fit through, leads to the backyard.
name: Wooden Barn

desc:
A large paddock is hemmed in by a sturdy fence, the ground is exposed on the
edges where farm animals pace, and in the middle is green grass and ordure. In
the northeast corner is a feed trough and a pile of hay. Near the entrance is a
watering trough. A heap of dung and other compost stands in the southwest corner
of the paddock and an iron gate blocks passage to the field in the east.
name: Barnyard

desc:
This barnyard is well-grazed, being mostly bare dirt with clumps of short,
sturdy grass. A heap of dung and food compost stands in the northwest corner of
the field and a trough of water sits in the middle, freshly tarred. The smell of
animals is strong and flies swarm the air.
name: Barnyard

desc:
The southeast corner of the barnyard is fenced off to form a pig pen. On the
near side of the pen is a feeding trough with scraps of rejected food. The
bottom of this pen is thick with mud, muck, and small bones, and the air is ripe
with offal.
name: Pig Pen

desc:
Cattle stalls line the western wall of this old, sturdy barn. The floor is lain
with smooth interlocking, natural stones, and decades of hard muck fill the
cracks. Next to the stalls is an assortment of buckets and milking stools, and
the air is rich with ordure and animal feed.
name: Sturdy Barn

desc:
Built as an extension to the barn, this small chicken coop has rows of nests
stacked on top of each other. Hay lines the hard dirt floor, and the walls are
hard slats, letting in air and light. Flies and gnats fly about in the air,
dodging feathers and hay motes.
name: Chicken Coop

desc:
Bales of hay fill this yawning loft. The smell of their dried grasses fills the
air, partially masking the smell of animals from below. The old, dry, dark
wooden walls rise high and hold a great many bales of hay. Near the roof peak,
unreachable spider webs hang full of ancient dead bugs.
name: Hay Loft

desc:
The entrance to this vast cavern is dark and damp. A musty earth smell fills the
humid air, making it a little harder to breathe. The walls are moist, and the
ground very muddy. In the ceiling, a large hole can be seen with exposed tree
roots protruding from the wall.
name: Vast Cavern

desc:
This tunnel is relatively narrow and small in size, making it feel a little
cramped. Sand and earth have settled, sealing what used to be cracks covering
the wall. Dark grey stone makes up most of the tunnel, but here and there veins
of brighter granite shimmer in the darkness.
name: Narrow Tunnel

desc:
Moisture has gathered here, causing droplets of water on the walls and the
ceiling of this small tunnel to drip to the floor. Little puddles of dark water
have gathered to form in shallow depressions on the floor. A cramped, narrow
tunnel leads off into the darkness in the north.
name: Narrow Tunnel

desc:
The walls of this cave are crystalline and are composed of many small fractals
of colour. Judging by the bones and decomposing remains on the floor, some wild
creatures have discovered it and decided to make their home here. The cave
continues deeper below ground level.
name: Crystal Cave

desc:
This small cave is littered with rocks and boulders. The temperature in here,
below ground level, is high and the air is humid. A faint breeze of fresh air
can be felt coming from above where the cave walls are covered with crystals.
name: Bear Cave

desc:
A quiet wood follows the road to the west. To the east, there is a building.
Through the window, the charming prospect of a bar can be seen, with rows of
green bottles and gold labels, together with jars of pickles and preserves, and
cheeses and boiled hams, and rounds of beef. A sign above the door invites
travellers in and tells its name: The Nasty Mewlip's Inn.
name: In Front of the Nasty Mewlip's Inn

desc:
This dim room, only sparsely lit by candles on small wooden tables, is filled
with the smell of fresh beer, bread, and the melting candles. The shelf behind
the bar is stocked with a fine choice of liquors. A notice, painted on a wooden
board, is hanging above the bar.
name: The Nasty Mewlip's Inn

desc:
Tall grass here hides glints of rotten food and discarded cups, presumably left
by patrons from the inn far to the west. The smell of rot permeates the air
here, and insects can be seen crawling through the grass, no doubt attracted by
the smell of decomposition.
name: Grassy Field
